cards.front: When work is done on a gas, what happens to its internal energy, and what may happen to its temperature?
cards.back: Its internal energy increases, and its temperature can increase.
cards.explanation: Doing work on the gas increases its internal energy. A temperature increase is possible rather than guaranteed.

choices.text: Its internal energy increases, and its temperature can increase.
choices.feedback: Correct. Work done on the gas increases its internal energy and can increase its temperature.

choices.text: Its internal energy decreases, and its temperature can decrease.
choices.feedback: Doing work on the gas increases, rather than decreases, its internal energy.

choices.text: Its internal energy stays constant, although its temperature can increase.
choices.feedback: The internal energy does not stay constant; it increases.

cards.sourceExcerpt:
Doing work on a gas increases the internal energy of the gas and
can cause an increase in the temperature of the gas.
cards.sourceLocator: Official PDF pages 36-36; 4.3.3.3 Increasing the pressure of a gas (physics only) (HT only)

cards.front: How do the height of the liquid column above a point and the liquid’s density affect pressure at that point?
cards.back: Pressure increases with both the height of liquid above the point and the liquid’s density.
cards.explanation: A taller column above the point and a denser liquid each increase the pressure there.

choices.text: Pressure decreases with both the height of liquid above the point and the liquid’s density.
choices.feedback: Pressure increases, rather than decreases, with both factors.

choices.text: Pressure increases with both the height of liquid above the point and the liquid’s density.
choices.feedback: Correct. Both greater column height and greater density increase pressure.

choices.text: Pressure increases with the height of liquid above the point but is independent of the liquid’s density.
choices.feedback: Density is also a stated factor: pressure increases with it.

cards.sourceExcerpt:
Students should be able to explain why, in a liquid, pressure at a
point increases with the height of the column of liquid above that
point and with the density of the liquid.
cards.sourceLocator: Official PDF pages 49-58; 4.5.5.1.2 Pressure in a fluid 2 (HT only)

cards.front: State conservation of momentum for an event in a closed system.
cards.back: In a closed system, total momentum before an event equals total momentum after it.
cards.explanation: The equality applies to the system’s total momentum and is called conservation of momentum.

choices.text: In a closed system, each object’s momentum is unchanged by an event.
choices.feedback: The law concerns total momentum, not the unchanged momentum of each object.

choices.text: In a closed system, total momentum before an event equals total momentum after it.
choices.feedback: Correct. The total momentum is equal before and after the event.

choices.text: In a closed system, total momentum after an event must be zero.
choices.feedback: The final total equals the initial total; it is not necessarily zero.

cards.sourceExcerpt:
In a closed system, the total momentum before an event is equal to
the total momentum after the event.
This is called conservation of momentum.

cards.front: What is the frequency range of normal human hearing?
cards.explanation: AQA gives 20 Hz to 20 kHz as the range of normal human hearing.

choices.text: 2 Hz to 2 kHz.
choices.feedback: Both limits are too low; the stated range begins at 20 Hz and ends at 20 kHz.

choices.text: 20 Hz to 20 kHz.
choices.feedback: Correct. This is the stated range of normal human hearing.

choices.text: 20 kHz to 200 kHz.
choices.feedback: Normal human hearing ends at 20 kHz rather than beginning there.

cards.sourceExcerpt:
Students should know that the range of normal human hearing is
from 20 Hz to 20 kHz.
cards.sourceLocator: Official PDF pages 62-62; 4.6.1.4 Sound waves (physics only) (HT only)

cards.front: How do P-waves and S-waves differ in wave type and ability to travel through liquids?
cards.back: P-waves are longitudinal and travel through solids and liquids; S-waves are transverse and cannot travel through a liquid.
cards.explanation: P-waves travel through solids and liquids at different speeds, whereas S-waves cannot travel through a liquid. Both provide evidence about the Earth’s core.

choices.text: P-waves are transverse and cannot travel through a liquid; S-waves are longitudinal and travel through solids and liquids.
choices.feedback: The properties are reversed: P-waves are longitudinal, while S-waves are transverse and cannot travel through liquid.

choices.text: P-waves are longitudinal and travel through solids and liquids; S-waves are transverse and cannot travel through a liquid.
choices.feedback: Correct. This contrast helps seismic waves provide evidence about the Earth’s core.

choices.text: P-waves and S-waves are both longitudinal and both travel through solids and liquids.
choices.feedback: S-waves are transverse and cannot travel through a liquid.

cards.sourceExcerpt:
Seismic waves are produced by earthquakes. P-waves are
longitudinal, seismic waves. P-waves travel at different speeds
through solids and liquids. S-waves are transverse, seismic waves.
S-waves cannot travel through a liquid. P-waves and S-waves
provide evidence for the structure and size of the Earth’s core.
cards.sourceLocator: Official PDF pages 62-62; 4.6.1.5 Waves for detection and exploration (physics only) (HT only)

cards.front: Which three quantities have their relative orientations represented by Fleming’s left-hand rule?
cards.back: The force, the current in the conductor and the magnetic field.
cards.explanation: Fleming’s left-hand rule represents the relative orientation of force, current and magnetic field.

choices.text: The force, the potential difference across the conductor and the magnetic field.
choices.feedback: Current in the conductor is represented, not potential difference.

choices.text: The force, the current in the conductor and the magnetic field.
choices.feedback: Correct. These are the three quantities whose relative orientations the rule represents.

choices.text: The force, the current in the conductor and the electric field.
choices.feedback: The rule represents the magnetic field, not an electric field.

cards.sourceExcerpt:
Students should be able to show that Fleming's left-hand rule
represents the relative orientation of the force, the current in the
conductor and the magnetic field.
cards.sourceLocator: Official PDF pages 69-72; 4.7.2.2 Fleming's left-hand rule (HT only)

cards.front: What effect forms the basis of an electric motor?
cards.back: A current-carrying coil in a magnetic field tends to rotate.
cards.explanation: The tendency of a current-carrying coil to rotate in a magnetic field is the stated basis of an electric motor.

choices.text: A current-carrying coil tends to rotate even without a magnetic field.
choices.feedback: The stated motor basis includes a magnetic field as well as a current-carrying coil.

choices.text: A coil in a magnetic field tends to rotate even when no current flows.
choices.feedback: The stated motor basis includes current in the coil as well as a magnetic field.

choices.text: A current-carrying coil in a magnetic field tends to rotate.
choices.feedback: Correct. This turning effect is the basis of an electric motor.

cards.sourceExcerpt:
A coil of wire carrying a current in a magnetic field tends to rotate.
This is the basis of an electric motor.
cards.sourceLocator: Official PDF pages 69-72; 4.7.2.3 Electric motors (HT only)

cards.front: What conversion do loudspeakers and headphones make using the motor effect?
cards.back: They use the motor effect to convert variations in current into pressure variations in sound waves.
cards.explanation: The conversion runs from electrical-current variations to sound-pressure variations, and its named mechanism is the motor effect.

choices.text: They use the generator effect to convert pressure variations in sound waves into variations in current.
choices.feedback: This reverses the stated conversion; loudspeakers start with variations in current.

choices.text: They use the motor effect to convert variations in current into pressure variations in sound waves.
choices.feedback: Correct. Current variations are converted into sound-wave pressure variations.

choices.text: They use the generator effect to convert variations in current into pressure variations in sound waves.
choices.feedback: The conversion is attributed to the motor effect, not the generator effect.

cards.sourceExcerpt:
Loudspeakers and headphones use the motor effect to convert
variations in current in electrical circuits to the pressure variations in
sound waves.
cards.sourceLocator: Official PDF pages 69-72; 4.7.2.4 Loudspeakers (physics only) (HT only)

cards.front: What does the magnetic field generated by an induced current do relative to the original change?
cards.back: It opposes the original change, whether that change is conductor movement or a change in magnetic field.
cards.explanation: The induced current’s magnetic field opposes either form of original change named: conductor movement or magnetic-field change.

choices.text: It reinforces the original change, whether that change is conductor movement or a change in magnetic field.
choices.feedback: The generated magnetic field opposes rather than reinforces the original change.

choices.text: It opposes the original change, whether that change is conductor movement or a change in magnetic field.
choices.feedback: Correct. The induced current’s field opposes the original change.

choices.text: It opposes conductor movement but not a change in magnetic field.
choices.feedback: The opposition applies to either conductor movement or a change in magnetic field.

cards.sourceExcerpt:
An induced current generates a magnetic field that opposes the
original change, either the movement of the conductor or the
change in magnetic field.
cards.sourceLocator: Official PDF pages 69-72; 4.7.3 Induced potential, transformers and the National Grid (physics only) (HT only)

cards.front: What changes induce a potential difference across a conductor, and when is a current induced?
cards.back: Relative motion between a conductor and a magnetic field, or a change in the field around the conductor, induces a potential difference; if the conductor is in a complete circuit, a current is induced.
cards.explanation: Relative movement or a changing magnetic field induces a potential difference. If the conductor forms part of a complete circuit, a current is induced; this is the generator effect.

choices.text: A conductor held still in an unchanging magnetic field induces a potential difference; an incomplete circuit then produces a current.
choices.feedback: The stated causes are relative motion or magnetic-field change, and the circuit must be complete for an induced current.

choices.text: Relative motion between a conductor and a magnetic field, or a change in the field around the conductor, induces a potential difference; if the conductor is in a complete circuit, a current is induced.
choices.feedback: Correct. These are the stated conditions for the generator effect.

choices.text: Only a complete circuit is needed to induce both a potential difference and a current; no motion or field change is required.
choices.feedback: A complete circuit allows induced current, but relative motion or field change induces the potential difference.

cards.sourceExcerpt:
If an electrical conductor moves relative to a magnetic field or if
there is a change in the magnetic field around a conductor, a
potential difference is induced across the ends of the conductor. If
the conductor is part of a complete circuit, a current is induced in
the conductor. This is called the generator effect.
cards.sourceLocator: Official PDF pages 69-72; 4.7.3.1 Induced potential (HT only)

cards.front: Using the generator effect, what type of current is generated by an alternator and by a dynamo?
cards.back: An alternator generates ac; a dynamo generates dc.
cards.explanation: The generator effect is used for both devices, with an alternator producing ac and a dynamo producing dc.

choices.text: An alternator generates ac; a dynamo generates dc.
choices.feedback: Correct. This is the stated alternator–dynamo distinction.

choices.text: An alternator generates dc; a dynamo generates ac.
choices.feedback: The outputs are reversed: the alternator generates ac and the dynamo generates dc.

choices.text: An alternator and a dynamo both generate dc.
choices.feedback: Only the dynamo is stated to generate dc; the alternator generates ac.

cards.sourceExcerpt:
The generator effect is used in an alternator to generate ac and in a
dynamo to generate dc.
cards.sourceLocator: Official PDF pages 69-72; 4.7.3.2 Uses of the generator effect (HT only)

cards.front: What conversion does a microphone make using the generator effect?
cards.back: It uses the generator effect to convert pressure variations in sound waves into variations in current in electrical circuits.
cards.explanation: A microphone’s conversion runs from sound-wave pressure variations to electrical-current variations, using the generator effect.

choices.text: It uses the motor effect to convert variations in current into pressure variations in sound waves.
choices.feedback: This reverses the microphone conversion; the microphone starts with sound-wave pressure variations.

choices.text: It uses the motor effect to convert pressure variations in sound waves into variations in current in electrical circuits.
choices.feedback: The conversion direction is right, but the named mechanism is the generator effect.

choices.text: It uses the generator effect to convert pressure variations in sound waves into variations in current in electrical circuits.
choices.feedback: Correct. Sound-pressure variations are converted into current variations.

cards.sourceExcerpt:
Microphones use the generator effect to convert the pressure
variations in sound waves into variations in current in electrical
circuits.

cards.front: What does a basic transformer consist of, and why is iron used for its core?
cards.back: A primary coil and a secondary coil wound on an iron core; iron is used because it is easily magnetised.
cards.explanation: Both coils are wound on the iron core, whose stated relevant property is that it is easily magnetised.

choices.text: A primary coil and a secondary coil wound on an iron core; iron is used because it is easily magnetised.
choices.feedback: Correct. A basic transformer has two coils on an easily magnetised iron core.

choices.text: One coil wound on an iron core; iron is used because it is easily magnetised.
choices.feedback: A basic transformer has both a primary coil and a secondary coil.

choices.text: A primary coil and a secondary coil wound on a copper core; copper is used because it is easily magnetised.
choices.feedback: The stated core material is iron, chosen because it is easily magnetised.

cards.sourceExcerpt:
A basic transformer consists of a primary coil and a secondary coil
wound on an iron core.
Iron is used as it is easily magnetised.
